content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 14, 2019

Lacei Amodei
31540 Burnham Way
Hayward, CA 94544

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-19-192

Dear Ms. Amodei:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating
your inquiry as a request for informal assistance.²

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. May a recipient committee be controlled by two or more candidates and if so, what are the
Recipient Campaign Statement Form 410 ("Statement of Organization"), Statement of Intention
Form 501 ("Statement of Intention"), and Recipient Committee Campaign Statement Form 460
("Campaign Statement") filing requirements for the committee?

2. If it is permissible to have more than two candidates controlling the recipient committee, is it
permissible to add a third candidate by filing an amendment?

3. What are the campaign contribution limits if we have two or more candidates controlling the
recipient committee?

4. Does the home or office fundraiser exemption allow a host, including a candidate, to spend
$500 on supplies related to hosting the event?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-19-192
Page No. 2

5. What are your reporting requirements if an individual or organization endorses your candidacy?

CONCLUSIONS

1-3. Although we cannot advise on past conduct, there is nothing in the Act that prohibits multiple
local candidates from controlling one committee. Additionally, at this time the Act does not
contain contribution limits for local committees. However, a local ordinance may restrict such
activity. We do not advise on the applicability of local contribution limits for your jurisdiction.
Information on local contribution limits can be found in your local ordinance.

4. Yes. The costs for a home or office fundraiser are not considered a contribution to the
committee if they do not exceed $500.

5. An endorsement of a candidate may become a contribution or an independent expenditure when
a payment is made in connection with the endorsement. A payment made by a candidate for a
communication publicizing his or her endorsement by another candidate, provided that the
communication does not expressly advocate the nomination or election of the endorsing
candidate or the defeat of an endorsing candidate's opponent is not a contribution.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a candidate for the Hayward City Council for the 2020 election. You and another
candidate have formed a recipient committee "Amodei & Crader for Hayward City Council 2020."
You are requesting advice on five separate questions relating to your committee. You have filed one
joint Statement of Organization with the other candidate and you and the other candidate
individually have each filed a separate Statement of Intention. You would like to confirm whether it
is permissible to have two or more local candidates controlling one recipient committee.
Additionally, if it is permissible to have more than two candidates, you would like to confirm
whether it is permissible to add a third local candidate by filing an amendment to the committee's
Statement of Organization. Further, you'd like advice on the home or office fundraiser exemption
and would like to know whether the host of an event can spend up to $500 on supplies relating to
the event without the expenses counting as a campaign contribution. Lastly, you would like advice
on your reporting requirements if an individual or organization endorses your candidacy.

ANALYSIS

Committee Filing Requirements

Although we cannot advise on past conduct,³ the Act does not prohibit multiple local
candidates from controlling a committee. However, this conduct may be restricted by a local
ordinance. The Act does not contain contribution limits for local candidates but provides that cities
and counties may adopt contribution limits applicable to their elections. (Section 85703.) Many
California cities and counties have adopted campaign ordinances that include contribution limits
and other disclosure provisions. For questions about local restrictions, candidates and committees
should contact their city clerk, county elections office, or their City Attorney's or County Counsel's

³ See Regulation 18329(b)(6)(A).

File No. I-19-192
Page No. 3

office. The Commission does not advise on local ordinance provisions applicable in your
jurisdiction.

Contributions

Home or Office Fundraising Exemption

"Contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third
party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment, except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received or if it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment is not
made for political purposes. (Section 82015.)

Section 82015 also provides and exemption, stating that a contribution does not include any
of the following: … "(2) Except as provided in paragraph (5) of subdivision (b), a payment made by
an occupant of a home or office for costs related to any meeting or fundraising event held in the
occupant's home or office if the costs for the meeting or fundraising event are five hundred dollars
($500) or less." (Section 82015.)

In further support, Regulation 18215 provides that a payment made by an occupant of a
home or office, other than a lobbyist or lobbying firm, for costs related to any meeting or
fundraising event held in the occupant's home or office, if the total cost of the meeting or
fundraising event is $500 or less, exclusive of the fair rental value of the premises is not a
contribution. (Regulation 18215.)

The exemption set forth in Section 82015 and Regulation 18215 permits campaign
volunteers to hold low-cost neighborhood coffees, meet and greets, and fundraisers in their home or
office for committees under $500 without the event counting as a reportable contribution to the
committee. (Olson Advice Letter, No. I-15-065; Crane Advice Letter, No. A-15-233.) This includes·
goods and services provided by the candidate. (Van Vleck Advice Letter, No. A-09-149.) If the cost
of the event exceeds $500, all payments are counted as contributions. (Ibid.)

You have asked whether the exemption allows a host, including a candidate, to spend $500
on supplies related to hosting the event. Supplies provided for a home fundraiser fall within this
exemption so long as the costs do not exceed $500.

Endorsements

The Act does not contain any provisions restricting from whom a candidate may receive an
endorsement.⁴ In addition, an endorsement itself does not constitute a "contribution" to a candidate.
Under the Act, a contribution is defined as any payment made for political purposes for which full
and adequate consideration is not received. (Sections 82015 and 82044; Regulation 18215.) A
payment is made for political purposes if it is received by or made at the behest of a candidate.
(Section 82015; Regulation 18215.) Because an endorsement is not a "payment,"
an endorsement by itself is not considered a "contribution" to a candidate's campaign. (Starrh
Advice Letter, No. I-10-2015.) However, an endorsement of a candidate may become a contribution

⁴ Agency policies or other laws outside the Act may affect whether a public agency may endorse a candidate.

File No. 1-19-192
Page No. 4

or an independent expenditure when a payment is made in connection with the endorsement. For
example, we advised in the Starrh Advice Letter that if an agency makes a payment to publicize its
endorsement of a candidate in a newspaper advertisement, the payment will be considered a
contribution to the candidate if it is made "at the behest" of the candidate or an independent
expenditure. A payment is made "at the behest" of a candidate if it is made under the control or at
the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request of or
suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of the candidate. (Regulation 18225.7.)

Also please note that if a candidate pays for a communication that supports another
candidate, and the payment is made at the behest of the endorsed candidate, the payment is not
considered to be an independent expenditure if: (1) the candidate paying for the communication
also is included in the communication; (2) the non-paying candidate is listed in the same ballot as
the paying candidate; and (3) the communications is targeted only to potential voters in the paying
candidate's district. (In re St. Croix (2005) 18 FPPC Ops. 1.)

You have only asked for general information relating to endorsements and have not
provided any specific facts regarding an endorsement to your candidacy. As a result, we only
provide general information and examples relating to endorsements.⁵

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Katelyn Greene
Counsel, Legal Division

KG:sal

⁵ For additional information and examples of when an endorsement may become a contribution or an
independent expenditure, see Campaign Manual 2, Chapter 6.
